categories.summary: Train the decision habits that keep trading from turning into guessing.

sections.label: 01 Survival
sections.title: 01 Your First Goal Is Survival
sections.completion_signal: You can apply this mindset rule in a player-facing market decision.

pages.heading: Learning Objective
pages.body: Players understand that the first goal in the market is not maximum profit, but survival. Without survival, players cannot learn, compound, or recover from mistakes.

pages.heading: Key Concept
pages.body: The market rewards players who stay in the game long enough to improve their process.

pages.heading: Knowledge Card
pages.body: Before thinking about profit, protect your ability to keep playing. A player who loses all capital because of one emotional decision does not get to benefit from future opportunities.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario: The player receives starting capital. A trending stock is rising fast, but the player has not yet built a safety buffer.
checks.question: What is the best decision before taking this fast-moving trade?

options.label: Do not go all-in; keep enough cash or safety buffer and only risk money that can handle volatility.
options.feedback: Correct. Survival creates more decision cycles.

options.label: Go all-in because the trend is already moving.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Ignore the safety buffer and decide after the trade.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Trade every day until the account doubles.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objective
content_blocks.body: Players understand that the first goal in the market is not maximum profit, but survival. Without survival, players cannot learn, compound, or recover from mistakes.

content_blocks.heading: Key Concept

content_blocks.heading: Knowledge Card
content_blocks.body: Before thinking about profit, protect your ability to keep playing. A player who loses all capital because of one emotional decision does not get to benefit from future opportunities.

content_blocks.title: Mindset Summary
bullets: Survival creates more decision cycles.
All-in decisions magnify financial and emotional risk.
The Risk Meter should rise when hidden danger increases.

sections.label: 02 Cold Money
sections.title: 02 Money Readiness: Is This Money Ready for the Market?
sections.completion_signal: You can apply this mindset rule in a player-facing market decision.

pages.heading: Learning Objective
pages.body: Players understand that not all money is investable. Money for daily needs, debt, rent, or emergencies should not be treated the same as cold money.

pages.heading: Key Concept
pages.body: Investing becomes emotionally dangerous when the money has a near-term survival purpose.

pages.heading: Knowledge Card
pages.body: Before investing, the player should have basic income, covered living needs, and an emergency buffer. Money with urgent real-life purpose often leads to panic decisions when prices fall.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario: The player has Rp10,000,000. Monthly expenses are Rp4,000,000. Emergency buffer is zero. A stock is trending.
checks.question: What should the player prioritize before taking large market risk?

options.label: Build or protect the emergency buffer first, then invest only the portion that can tolerate temporary loss.
options.feedback: Correct. Cold money can tolerate volatility.

options.label: Use the whole Rp10,000,000 because the stock is trending.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Borrow more money to avoid missing the move.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Treat the emergency buffer as spare trading cash.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

content_blocks.title: Mindset Summary
bullets: Cold money can tolerate volatility.
Emergency funds should stay outside risky trades.
Using bill-payment money should increase risk pressure.

sections.label: 03 Goals
sections.title: 03 Why Invest: Future Self, Inflation, and Compounding
sections.completion_signal: You can apply this mindset rule in a player-facing market decision.

pages.heading: Learning Objective
pages.body: Players understand the purpose of investing: growing wealth, preparing for the future, earning passive income, and protecting purchasing power.

pages.heading: Key Concept
pages.body: Investing is not only about short-term profit. It is a tool to serve future goals.

pages.heading: Knowledge Card
pages.body: A good player connects market decisions to a long-term goal: education, home, retirement, passive income, inflation protection, or wealth growth.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario: The player chooses a goal before starting: short-term challenge, future house, retirement, passive income, or skill-building.
checks.question: What is the best first step before choosing market risk?

options.label: Pick a goal, because goal clarity affects time horizon and risk tolerance.
options.feedback: Correct. Goals turn random buying into filtered decisions.

options.label: Skip the goal and follow the highest daily mover.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Choose the riskiest stock because compounding needs speed.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Change the goal every time the portfolio turns red.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objective
content_blocks.body: Players understand the purpose of investing: growing wealth, preparing for the future, earning passive income, and protecting purchasing power.

content_blocks.heading: Key Concept
content_blocks.body: Investing is not only about short-term profit. It is a tool to serve future goals.

content_blocks.heading: Knowledge Card
content_blocks.body: A good player connects market decisions to a long-term goal: education, home, retirement, passive income, inflation protection, or wealth growth.

content_blocks.title: Mindset Summary
bullets: Goals turn random buying into filtered decisions.
Time horizon shapes how much volatility the player can tolerate.
Compounding works best when destructive drawdowns are avoided.

sections.label: 04 Ownership
sections.title: 04 Stock Is Business Ownership
sections.completion_signal: You can apply this mindset rule in a player-facing market decision.

pages.heading: Learning Objective
pages.body: Players understand that buying a stock means owning a piece of a business, not just buying a moving ticker.

pages.heading: Key Concept
pages.body: A stock price is easier to understand when the player asks: What business am I buying?

pages.heading: Knowledge Card
pages.body: Behind every stock is a company with assets, products, management, cash flow, debt, and future profit potential. Price movement matters, but business quality matters too.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
The player sees three stocks:

| Stock | Price Action | Business Read | Driver |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stock A | Up fast | Weakening | Excitement |
| Stock B | Flat | Improving | Business progress |
| Stock C | Up | Unknown | Rumor only |
checks.question: Which stock deserves deeper investigation first?

options.label: Investigate Stock B first, because business quality can matter more than short-term excitement.
options.feedback: Correct. A ticker is a door; behind it is a business.

options.label: Buy Stock A immediately because price is already up.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Buy Stock C because rumor can move faster than facts.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Ignore business quality and choose the greenest candle.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objective
content_blocks.body: Players understand that buying a stock means owning a piece of a business, not just buying a moving ticker.

content_blocks.heading: Key Concept
content_blocks.body: A stock price is easier to understand when the player asks: What business am I buying?

content_blocks.heading: Knowledge Card
content_blocks.body: Behind every stock is a company with assets, products, management, cash flow, debt, and future profit potential. Price movement matters, but business quality matters too.

content_blocks.title: Mindset Summary
bullets: A ticker is a door; behind it is a business.
Price can move daily while business quality takes longer to prove.
Business-owner mindset asks what the company does before asking what the candle did.

sections.label: 05 Price Drivers
sections.title: 05 How Stock Prices Move: Business Growth vs Sentiment
sections.completion_signal: You can apply this mindset rule in a player-facing market decision.

pages.heading: Learning Objective
pages.body: Players understand that stock prices can move because of real business growth or because of sentiment, hype, rumors, macro changes, or corporate actions.

pages.heading: Key Concept
pages.body: Not every price increase means the business improved.

pages.heading: Knowledge Card
pages.body: A stock can rise because profits improve, assets grow, the sector becomes attractive, or management executes well. It can also rise because of hype, rumor, or temporary sentiment. The player must learn to separate signal from noise.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario: A company reports strong revenue growth, but net profit falls. Meanwhile, the market is hyping the stock. The player must decide whether the move is supported by fundamentals.
checks.question: What should the player do when price action and business data disagree?

options.label: Do not rely on price movement alone; compare business data, sentiment, and risk before deciding.
options.feedback: Correct. Business growth can be durable but still needs verification.

options.label: Buy automatically because revenue growth is strong.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Sell automatically because net profit fell.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Ignore the business data because the crowd is excited.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objective
content_blocks.body: Players understand that stock prices can move because of real business growth or because of sentiment, hype, rumors, macro changes, or corporate actions.

content_blocks.heading: Key Concept
content_blocks.body: Not every price increase means the business improved.

content_blocks.heading: Knowledge Card
content_blocks.body: A stock can rise because profits improve, assets grow, the sector becomes attractive, or management executes well. It can also rise because of hype, rumor, or temporary sentiment. The player must learn to separate signal from noise.

content_blocks.title: Mindset Summary
bullets: Business growth can be durable but still needs verification.
Sentiment can move price quickly and reverse quickly.
When price and business data disagree, investigate before deciding.

sections.label: 06 Identity
sections.title: 06 Investor, Trader, or Speculator?
sections.completion_signal: You can apply this mindset rule in a player-facing market decision.

pages.heading: Learning Objective
pages.body: Players can distinguish investor, trader, and speculator behavior.

pages.heading: Key Concept
pages.body: Different playstyles are valid, but mixing them without awareness creates confusion and risk.

pages.heading: Knowledge Card
pages.body: Investors focus on business quality and long-term value. Traders focus on shorter-term price movement and technical setups. Speculators chase rumor or hype without enough analysis.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario: A stock rises 20% after a rumor. The player must choose how to respond based on their selected playstyle.
checks.question: What is the strongest response to the rumor-driven move?

options.label: Pick one logic path: investor checks business and valuation, trader checks setup and exit, speculator recognizes high risk and sizes very small or avoids.
options.feedback: Correct. Investor logic checks business and value.

options.label: Enter as a trader, then become an investor if the trade fails.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Buy first and choose the playstyle later.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Treat rumor, business quality, and technical setup as identical signals.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objective
content_blocks.body: Players can distinguish investor, trader, and speculator behavior.

content_blocks.heading: Key Concept
content_blocks.body: Different playstyles are valid, but mixing them without awareness creates confusion and risk.

content_blocks.heading: Knowledge Card
content_blocks.body: Investors focus on business quality and long-term value. Traders focus on shorter-term price movement and technical setups. Speculators chase rumor or hype without enough analysis.

content_blocks.title: Mindset Summary
bullets: Investor logic checks business and value.
Trader logic checks setup, trend, volume, and exit plan.
Speculation should be recognized as high risk and sized very small or avoided.

sections.label: 07 Toolkit
sections.title: 07 Analysis Toolkit: Fundamental, Technical, Money Flow, and Value
sections.completion_signal: You can apply this mindset rule in a player-facing market decision.

pages.heading: Learning Objective
pages.body: Players understand the main analysis approaches and when each is useful.

pages.heading: Key Concept
pages.body: Different tools answer different questions.

pages.heading: Knowledge Card
pages.body: Fundamental analysis asks whether the business is healthy. Technical analysis studies historical price and volume patterns. Money flow analysis looks at large fund movement. Value investing compares current price to estimated business value.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
The player receives four tasks:

| Task | Needed Read |
|---|---|
| Check business health | Company quality |
| Find a possible entry zone | Price and chart setup |
| Detect unusual volume | Flow behavior |
| Judge whether price is below value | Price versus worth |
checks.question: What should the player do with these four tasks?

options.label: Match each task to the right tool: fundamental, technical, money flow, and value analysis.
options.feedback: Correct. Fundamental analysis checks business health.

options.label: Use only rumors for every task.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Use technical analysis to judge all business quality.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Use one tool blindly because it worked once.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objective
content_blocks.body: Players understand the main analysis approaches and when each is useful.

content_blocks.heading: Key Concept
content_blocks.body: Different tools answer different questions.

content_blocks.heading: Knowledge Card
content_blocks.body: Fundamental analysis asks whether the business is healthy. Technical analysis studies historical price and volume patterns. Money flow analysis looks at large fund movement. Value investing compares current price to estimated business value.

content_blocks.title: Mindset Summary
bullets: Fundamental analysis checks business health.
Technical analysis studies price patterns and indicators.
Money flow looks for large fund movement.
Value investing compares price with estimated business value.

sections.label: 08 Anti-FOMO
sections.title: 08 Framework: The Anti-FOMO Checklist
sections.completion_signal: You can apply this mindset rule in a player-facing market decision.

pages.heading: Learning Objective
pages.body: Players understand why they need a decision framework to avoid FOMO, overtrading, inconsistency, and impatience.

pages.heading: Key Concept
pages.body: A framework acts like a compass when the market is noisy.

pages.heading: Knowledge Card
pages.body: Without a framework, players can buy because of hype, trade too often, switch strategies randomly, or sell too early because of fear. A good framework slows down impulsive decisions.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario: A stock is up 35% in a week. Social feed says: Last chance! The player must pass a checklist before buying.
checks.question: What is the best first question before buying this fast-rising stock?

options.label: Why is it moving, and is the reason strong enough for the risk?
options.feedback: Correct. FOMO asks: Will I miss it?

options.label: Will I miss it if I wait?
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Who is shouting the loudest?
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Can I use all my cash before the price moves again?
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objective
content_blocks.body: Players understand why they need a decision framework to avoid FOMO, overtrading, inconsistency, and impatience.

content_blocks.heading: Key Concept
content_blocks.body: A framework acts like a compass when the market is noisy.

content_blocks.heading: Knowledge Card
content_blocks.body: Without a framework, players can buy because of hype, trade too often, switch strategies randomly, or sell too early because of fear. A good framework slows down impulsive decisions.

content_blocks.heading: Recommended checklist
content_blocks.body:
Before buying, ask:
1. Why is the stock moving?
2. Is the reason fundamental, technical, sentiment, or rumor?
3. What is the risk?
4. What would make the thesis wrong?
5. How much capital is appropriate?
6. What is the exit plan?

content_blocks.title: Mindset Summary
bullets: FOMO asks: Will I miss it?
Framework asks: Is the reason strong enough for the risk?
Overtrading is often FOMO in disguise.

sections.label: 09 Risk
sections.title: 09 Risk Is Part of the Game
sections.completion_signal: You can apply this mindset rule in a player-facing market decision.

pages.heading: Learning Objective
pages.body: Players understand different types of risk and how to respond to them.

pages.heading: Key Concept
pages.body: Risk cannot be eliminated. It must be recognized, sized, and managed.

pages.heading: Knowledge Card
pages.body: Risks include price volatility, company risk, low liquidity, macroeconomic risk, geopolitical risk, regulation, and rumor. A good player does not panic at every red candle; they ask whether the original reason for owning is still valid.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
The player owns a stock that falls 18%. News appears:

| New Evidence | Read |
|---|---|
| Profit margin decreased | Business pressure |
| Management says expansion is delayed | Execution delay |
| Sector is still strong | External support |
checks.question: What is the best first response to the 18% drop?

options.label: Review the thesis, catalyst, risk, and sizing to decide whether the thesis is intact, delayed, or broken.
options.feedback: Correct. A red candle is information, not an instruction.

options.label: Panic sell automatically because red means danger.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Buy more blindly because the sector is still strong.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Ignore the news and blame the market.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objective
content_blocks.body: Players understand different types of risk and how to respond to them.

content_blocks.heading: Key Concept
content_blocks.body: Risk cannot be eliminated. It must be recognized, sized, and managed.

content_blocks.heading: Knowledge Card
content_blocks.body: Risks include price volatility, company risk, low liquidity, macroeconomic risk, geopolitical risk, regulation, and rumor. A good player does not panic at every red candle; they ask whether the original reason for owning is still valid.

content_blocks.title: Mindset Summary
bullets: A red candle is information, not an instruction.
Risk is not the enemy; unmanaged risk is.
Thesis review should decide whether the idea is on track, delayed, or broken.

sections.label: 10 Value
sections.title: 10 Price vs Value and Margin of Safety
sections.completion_signal: You can apply this mindset rule in a player-facing market decision.

pages.heading: Learning Objective
pages.body: Players understand that cheap price is not always good value, and high price is not automatically bad value.

pages.heading: Key Concept
pages.body: Value is what the player gets. Price is what the player pays. Margin of safety gives room for error.

pages.heading: Knowledge Card
pages.body: A stock can look cheap but be a bad business. Another stock can look expensive but still be valuable if growth, quality, and future cash flow justify it. The player should buy with a margin of safety, not blind optimism.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
Two stocks appear:

| Stock | Price Impression | Business Read |
|---|---|---|
| Stock A | Low price | Declining business |
| Stock B | Higher price | Stronger growth and clearer future |
checks.question: Which stock deserves further analysis first?

options.label: Study Stock B first, while still checking valuation and margin of safety before any buy decision.
options.feedback: Correct. Price is what you pay; value is what you get.

options.label: Choose Stock A automatically because low price means cheap value.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Buy Stock B immediately without a value range.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Avoid analysis because price and value are always identical.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objective
content_blocks.body: Players understand that cheap price is not always good value, and high price is not automatically bad value.

content_blocks.heading: Key Concept
content_blocks.body: Value is what the player gets. Price is what the player pays. Margin of safety gives room for error.

content_blocks.heading: Knowledge Card
content_blocks.body: A stock can look cheap but be a bad business. Another stock can look expensive but still be valuable if growth, quality, and future cash flow justify it. The player should buy with a margin of safety, not blind optimism.

content_blocks.title: Mindset Summary
bullets: Price is what you pay; value is what you get.
Low price can be a value trap when the business is weak.
A value range is better than false precision.

sections.label: 11 Thesis
sections.title: 11 Build a Thesis Before You Buy
sections.completion_signal: You can apply this mindset rule in a player-facing market decision.

pages.heading: Learning Objective
pages.body: Players learn to write a simple investment or trade thesis before taking a position.

pages.heading: Key Concept
pages.body: Without a thesis, the player is borrowing someone else's conviction.

pages.heading: Knowledge Card
pages.body: A thesis explains why the player owns a stock, what must happen for the idea to work, what could go wrong, how long they plan to hold, and what would make them exit.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario: Before buying a stock, the player opens the thesis template and must decide whether the idea is ready for a position.
checks.question: What should the player do before entering the position?

options.label: Write a thesis that covers the company, catalyst, risks, invalidation, time horizon, and reasonable position size.
options.feedback: Correct. Conviction is built from understanding, not volume.

options.label: Buy first and invent the thesis after price moves.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Copy only a target price from chat.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Use a green chart screenshot as the whole thesis.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objective
content_blocks.body: Players learn to write a simple investment or trade thesis before taking a position.

content_blocks.heading: Key Concept
content_blocks.body: Without a thesis, the player is borrowing someone else's conviction.

content_blocks.heading: Knowledge Card
content_blocks.body: A thesis explains why the player owns a stock, what must happen for the idea to work, what could go wrong, how long they plan to hold, and what would make them exit.

content_blocks.heading: In-game thesis template
content_blocks.body:
Before buying, ask:
1. What does the company do?
2. Why might the price or business improve?
3. What is the catalyst?
4. What are the biggest risks?
5. What would prove me wrong?
6. What is my time horizon?
7. What position size is reasonable?

content_blocks.title: Mindset Summary
bullets: Conviction is built from understanding, not volume.
A thesis should explain both upside and failure points.
When facts change, review the thesis.

sections.label: 12 Portfolio
sections.title: 12 Portfolio Management: Sizing, Diversification, and Avoiding All-In
sections.completion_signal: You can apply this mindset rule in a player-facing market decision.

pages.heading: Learning Objective
pages.body: Players understand that portfolio success depends not only on choosing stocks, but also on sizing and risk distribution.

pages.heading: Key Concept
pages.body: A good idea can become a bad portfolio decision if position size is reckless.

pages.heading: Knowledge Card
pages.body: Portfolio management protects the player from being destroyed by one wrong idea. Diversification spreads risk. Position sizing controls emotional pressure. Avoiding all-in keeps the player flexible.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario: The player finds a strong idea and can allocate 5%, 20%, 50%, or 100% of capital. A surprise bad event appears. The player sees how different sizing changes the outcome.
checks.question: What is the healthiest portfolio decision?

options.label: Choose a survivable position size, keep diversification, and avoid putting the whole portfolio into one idea.
options.feedback: Correct. Do not let one stock decide the whole journey.

options.label: Allocate 100% because the idea looks strong.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Ignore sizing because good ideas cannot fail.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

options.label: Chase every trending stock so no opportunity is missed.
options.feedback: Not quite. The PDF mindset framework favors survival, evidence, sizing, and process before action.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objective
content_blocks.body: Players understand that portfolio success depends not only on choosing stocks, but also on sizing and risk distribution.

content_blocks.heading: Key Concept
content_blocks.body: A good idea can become a bad portfolio decision if position size is reckless.

content_blocks.heading: Knowledge Card
content_blocks.body: Portfolio management protects the player from being destroyed by one wrong idea. Diversification spreads risk. Position sizing controls emotional pressure. Avoiding all-in keeps the player flexible.

content_blocks.title: Mindset Summary
bullets: Do not let one stock decide the whole journey.
Position sizing is emotional risk management.
Diversification reduces dependence on one outcome.

sections.label: 13 Challenge
sections.title: 13 Final Challenge: The Viral Stock
sections.completion_signal: Score at least 80 percent to earn the Mindset Basics badge.

pages.heading: Final Challenge
pages.body: Apply the full mindset framework under hype, volatility, and uncertainty.

content_blocks.heading: Scenario
content_blocks.body:
A small-cap stock is going viral. The simulation shows:

- Price up 80% in two weeks.
- Influencers are talking about it.
- Rumor of acquisition.
- Low liquidity.
- No confirmed disclosure yet.
- Friend says: Trust me, this is the one.

content_blocks.heading: Player Tasks
content_blocks.body:
1. Identify the price driver.
2. Separate confirmed facts from rumor.
3. Choose playstyle: investor, trader, or speculator.
4. Write a short thesis.
5. Decide position size.
6. Define exit condition.
7. Respond to a sudden 25% drop.

content_blocks.heading: Recommended Scoring
content_blocks.body:
| Player Behavior | Score Impact |
|---|---:|
| Checks source of price movement | +15 Framework |
| Separates rumor from confirmed fact | +20 Framework |
| Uses appropriate position sizing | +20 Risk Control |
| Defines exit condition | +15 Discipline |
| Avoids using emergency buffer | +25 Survival |
| Buys only because of hype | +30 Noise |
| Goes all-in | +40 Risk |
| Changes strategy after losing | +20 Inconsistency |
