quiz_questions.prompt: What is the first thing to check in a viral stock?

options.label: Why the price is moving and whether the reason is confirmed
The comment with the most likes

quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Viral movement needs evidence-checking before action.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Start with the driver and confirmation status, not crowd excitement.

quiz_questions.prompt: A rumor is not confirmed. What should happen to position size?

options.label: It should be controlled or avoided

quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Unconfirmed catalysts carry high uncertainty.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Rumor requires smaller sizing or avoidance, especially when liquidity is weak.

quiz_questions.prompt: The stock drops 25% after entry. What is the best first response?

options.label: Review thesis, catalyst, risk, and exit plan

quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Review whether the thesis is intact, delayed, or broken.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: A sharp drop is information. Review the thesis and exit plan before acting.

quiz_questions.prompt: Which behavior indicates speculation?

options.label: Buying based only on rumor and hope

quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Speculation often lacks analysis and relies on hope.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Speculation is driven by rumor or hope without enough analysis.

quiz_questions.prompt: What is the best sign of a strong decision process?

options.label: Having a repeatable framework even when outcomes vary

quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Process quality matters because outcomes are uncertain.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Strong process is repeatable under uncertainty; it is not the same as always being right.

categories.label: Fundamental
categories.summary: Learn how to read business quality, financial statements, cash reality, and hidden risks.

badge.label: Fundamental Analyst
badge.description: Passed the Fundamental Academy financial statement exam.

sections.label: 01 Statements
sections.title: 01 Why Financial Statements Matter
sections.completion_signal: You can use why financial statements matter in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
The player sees three companies:

| Company | Net Profit | Cash Flow | Debt | Price Movement |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Company A | High | Negative | High | +30% |
| Company B | Moderate | Positive | Low | -5% |
| Company C | Low | Positive | Moderate | +3% |
checks.question: Which company deserves deeper investigation first?

options.label: Company B or C, because cash flow and debt quality deserve deeper review before chasing Company A.
options.feedback: Correct. Company A may be risky because profit is not supported by cash and debt is high; B or C deserves a cleaner business-quality read first.

options.label: Company A, because the price already jumped 30%.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Company A, because high net profit is enough on its own.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Skip all three because only price movement matters.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: Financial statements are the map and compass of a business. Price can move because of market emotion, but financial statements help players see whether a company is genuinely healthy, fragile, growing, or only cosmetically attractive.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
By the end of this lesson, players should be able to:

- Explain why investors should read financial statements before buying stocks.
- Understand the difference between auditor perspective and investor perspective.
- Identify the three main financial statements and what each one answers.

content_blocks.heading: Key Explanation
content_blocks.body:
Auditors focus on whether the report follows accounting standards and is fairly presented. Investors use the audited report as raw material to judge future prospects, risk, growth, and business quality.

The three main statements answer different questions:

| Statement | Main Question |
|---|---|
| Income Statement | Is the company making money? |
| Balance Sheet | What does the company own, owe, and keep for shareholders? |
| Cash Flow Statement | Is real cash coming in or going out? |

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: Financial statements are the map and compass of a business. Price can move because of market emotion, but financial statements help players see whether a company is genuinely healthy, fragile, growing, or only cosmetically attractive.
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 02 Three Pillars
sections.title: 02 The Three Financial Statement Pillars
sections.completion_signal: You can use the three financial statement pillars in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
A company reports:

- Revenue up 40%
- Net profit up 20%
- Operating cash flow negative
- Receivables up 80%
checks.question: What is the main concern in this report?

options.label: Sales may not be turning into cash, so receivables and cash collection need investigation.
options.feedback: Correct. Profit and revenue can look strong while cash reality weakens; all three statements must be read together.

options.label: The company is automatically healthy because revenue rose 40%.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: The balance sheet is irrelevant because net profit increased.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: The player should ignore cash flow until price falls.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: The three financial statements must be read together. Alone, each one can mislead. Together, they form a more complete picture.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Identify the function of Income Statement, Balance Sheet, and Cash Flow Statement.
- Understand why profit, assets, and cash are different.
- Recognize that "profitable" does not always mean "financially healthy."

content_blocks.heading: Key Explanation
content_blocks.body:
| Pillar | What It Shows | Investor Question |
|---|---|---|
| Income Statement | Business performance | Is the business profitable? |
| Balance Sheet | Financial position | Is the company financially strong? |
| Cash Flow Statement | Real cash movement | Does the business generate cash? |

A company can report profit but have weak cash flow. A company can own many assets but fund them with too much debt. A company can have high revenue but poor margins. That is why players must connect the puzzle pieces.

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: The three financial statements must be read together. Alone, each one can mislead. Together, they form a more complete picture.
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 03 Income Ladder
sections.title: 03 Income Statement: From Revenue to Net Profit
sections.completion_signal: You can use income statement: from revenue to net profit in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario: A coffee business sells drinks for Rp20,000 each. Direct production cost is Rp10,000, and operating cost per drink is Rp5,000.
checks.question: What are the gross profit and operating profit per drink?

options.label: Gross profit is Rp10,000 and operating profit is Rp5,000.
options.feedback: Correct. Revenue minus direct production cost gives gross profit; subtract operating cost to reach operating profit.

options.label: Gross profit is Rp20,000 and operating profit is Rp10,000.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Gross profit is Rp5,000 and operating profit is Rp10,000.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Gross profit is Rp10,000 and operating profit is Rp15,000.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: The Income Statement tells the story of how money enters the business and how much remains after each layer of cost.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Read the basic structure of an Income Statement.
- Understand revenue, COGS, gross profit, OPEX, operating profit, interest expense, tax, and net profit.
- Explain why the journey from revenue to net profit matters.

content_blocks.heading: Key Explanation
content_blocks.body:
Basic Income Statement flow:

Revenue
- Cost of Goods Sold
= Gross Profit
- Operating Expenses
+/- Other Income/Expenses
= Operating Profit
- Interest Expense
+ Income/Loss from Associates or JV
= Earnings Before Tax
- Taxes
= Net Profit

Each line tells a different part of the business story:

| Line Item | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Revenue | Money earned from selling products/services |
| COGS | Direct cost to produce goods/services |
| Gross Profit | Profit after direct production costs |
| OPEX | Cost to run the business daily |
| Operating Profit | Profit from core operations |
| Interest Expense | Cost of debt |
| Net Profit | Final profit after costs, interest, and tax |

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: The Income Statement tells the story of how money enters the business and how much remains after each layer of cost.
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 04 Revenue Growth
sections.title: 04 Revenue Growth: Real Growth or One-Time Spike?
sections.completion_signal: You can use revenue growth: real growth or one-time spike? in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
Two companies show 50% revenue growth.

| Company | Cause of Growth | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Company A | New stores, higher sales volume | Potentially sustainable |
| Company B | One-time commodity price spike | May reverse next year |
checks.question: Which company deserves the higher revenue sustainability score?

options.label: Company A, unless Company B has evidence of long-term structural demand.
options.feedback: Correct. Sustainable growth needs recurring drivers; a one-time price spike can reverse.

options.label: Company B, because any 50% growth is automatically better.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Both companies deserve the same score because the growth rate is equal.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Neither company should be reviewed because revenue growth is never useful.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: Revenue growth is not automatically good. Players must ask whether growth is consistent, sustainable, organic, and profitable.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Calculate revenue growth.
- Distinguish sustainable growth from one-time growth.
- Understand that historical growth does not guarantee future growth.
- Identify whether growth comes from volume, price, new market, acquisition, or commodity cycle.

content_blocks.heading: Formula
content_blocks.body: Revenue Growth = Ending Revenue / Beginning Revenue - 1

content_blocks.heading: Key Explanation
content_blocks.body:
Revenue can grow because:

- More units are sold
- Selling prices increase
- Company enters a new market
- Company launches a new product
- Company acquires another business
- Commodity price temporarily rises

Players should ask:

| Question | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| Is growth consistent? | Shows demand stability |
| Is growth organic or from acquisition? | Organic growth may be more repeatable |
| Is growth caused by commodity price? | Commodity growth can reverse quickly |
| Does growth create profit and cash? | Revenue without profit/cash is weak quality |

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: Revenue growth is not automatically good. Players must ask whether growth is consistent, sustainable, organic, and profitable.
Revenue Growth = Ending Revenue / Beginning Revenue - 1
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 05 Gross Margin
sections.title: 05 Gross Profit Margin: Pricing Power and Production Efficiency
sections.completion_signal: You can use gross profit margin: pricing power and production efficiency in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
A drink business sells at Rp20,000.

| Case | Cost to Make | Gross Profit | Gross Margin |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Case A | Rp10,000 | Rp10,000 | 50% |
| Case B | Rp15,000 | Rp5,000 | 25% |
checks.question: Which case has better gross economics?

options.label: Case A, because it keeps more gross profit from the same selling price.
options.feedback: Correct. Higher gross margin usually signals better pricing power or production efficiency before operating costs.

options.label: Case B, because higher production cost means higher quality automatically.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Both cases are equal because selling price is the same.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Case B, because lower gross margin is always safer.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: Gross Profit Margin shows how much profit remains after paying direct production costs. It helps players understand pricing power, supplier pressure, and production efficiency.

content_blocks.heading: Formula
content_blocks.body: Gross Profit Margin = Gross Profit / Revenue

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Calculate gross profit margin.
- Understand why margin differs by industry.
- Recognize when rising revenue hides weakening production economics.

content_blocks.heading: Key Explanation
content_blocks.body:
Gross margin is affected by:

- Selling price
- Raw material cost
- Production efficiency
- Supplier bargaining power
- Customer bargaining power
- Competition
- Brand strength
- Commodity exposure

High margin can mean strong brand, pricing power, efficient production, or low competition. Low margin is not always bad, but it usually means the company must rely on high volume, efficiency, or scale.

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: Gross Profit Margin shows how much profit remains after paying direct production costs. It helps players understand pricing power, supplier pressure, and production efficiency.
Gross Profit Margin = Gross Profit / Revenue
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 06 Profit Margins
sections.title: 06 Operating Profit Margin and Net Profit Margin
sections.completion_signal: You can use operating profit margin and net profit margin in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
| Company | Gross Margin | Operating Margin | Net Margin | Interpretation |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| A | 50% | 40% | 35% | Efficient and profitable |
| B | 50% | 10% | 3% | OPEX or below-operating costs may be heavy |
| C | 20% | 15% | 12% | Low gross margin but efficient operations |
checks.question: Which company needs investigation into OPEX or below-operating costs?

options.label: Company B.
options.feedback: Correct. Company B starts with strong gross margin but loses most of it before net profit, pointing to OPEX or below-operating pressure.

options.label: Company A.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Company C.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: All three have the same issue.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: Gross profit is not the final story. A business can have good gross margin but still weak operating or net margin if operating expenses, interest, tax, or other costs are too high.

content_blocks.heading: Formulas
content_blocks.body:
Operating Profit Margin = Operating Profit / Revenue
Net Profit Margin = Net Profit / Revenue

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Distinguish gross profit, operating profit, and net profit.
- Understand how OPEX, interest, and tax affect final profit.
- Identify whether profit is scalable or cost-heavy.

content_blocks.heading: Key Explanation
content_blocks.body:
Operating margin answers:

"After running the business day-to-day, how much profit remains?"

Net margin answers:

"After all costs, interest, and taxes, how much of revenue becomes final profit?"

A company with high gross margin but low operating margin may have heavy rent, salary, marketing, logistics, or admin costs. A company with high operating margin but low net margin may have large interest expenses or tax burden.

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: Gross profit is not the final story. A business can have good gross margin but still weak operating or net margin if operating expenses, interest, tax, or other costs are too high.
Operating Profit Margin = Operating Profit / Revenue
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 07 Profit Quality
sections.title: 07 Non-Recurring Items and Interest Coverage
sections.completion_signal: You can use non-recurring items and interest coverage in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario: A company reports net profit up 300%. Footnotes show most profit came from selling land. Operating profit was flat.
checks.question: What should the player do with the 300% net profit jump?

options.label: Mark it as a non-recurring gain and investigate core profit.
options.feedback: Correct. A land sale may boost reported profit once, but it does not prove repeatable business quality.

options.label: Upgrade business quality score immediately.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Assume future profit will also triple.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Buy all-in because the headline is strong.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: Not all profit is created equal. One-time gains can make a company look better than it really is. Interest coverage shows whether operating profit can cover debt interest.

content_blocks.heading: Formulas
content_blocks.body:
Interest Coverage Ratio = EBIT / Interest Expense

General guide:

| ICR | Interpretation |
|---:|---|
| < 1x | Danger zone |
| 1x-2x | Caution zone |
| Around 3x or higher | More comfortable, depending on industry |

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Identify non-recurring income/loss.
- Separate core profit from one-time profit.
- Evaluate whether debt interest is manageable.

content_blocks.heading: Key Explanation
content_blocks.body:
Non-recurring items include:

- Gain from selling assets or subsidiaries
- Unrealized gain from investment revaluation
- Forex gain/loss
- Debt restructuring gain
- One-time M&A gain
- Impairment loss

A large one-time gain can make net profit jump, but it may not happen again. Players should normalize earnings by focusing on recurring operating performance.

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: Not all profit is created equal. One-time gains can make a company look better than it really is. Interest coverage shows whether operating profit can cover debt interest.
Interest Coverage Ratio = EBIT / Interest Expense
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 08 Returns
sections.title: 08 ROE, ROA, EPS, and Profit Allocation
sections.completion_signal: You can use roe, roa, eps, and profit allocation in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario: Company A has ROE 20%, pays low dividends, and reinvests profit into expansion that keeps revenue and profit growing. Company B has ROE 8%, retains all earnings, but profit does not grow.
checks.question: Which company uses retained earnings better?

options.label: Company A.
options.feedback: Correct. Retained earnings are more useful when reinvestment produces attractive returns and growth.

options.label: Company B, because keeping all earnings is always better.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Both companies use capital equally well.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Neither can be judged using ROE or growth evidence.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: Profit is not enough. Players must know how efficiently the company uses capital, how much profit belongs to each share, and how management uses profit.

content_blocks.heading: Formulas
content_blocks.body:
ROE = Net Profit / Equity
ROA = Net Profit / Total Assets
EPS = Net Profit Attributable to Parent / Number of Shares
Dividend Payout Ratio = DPS / EPS

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Understand ROE as return on shareholder capital.
- Understand ROA as return on all assets.
- Understand EPS as profit per share.
- Evaluate whether retained earnings are used productively.
- Understand trade-offs between dividends, reinvestment, debt repayment, and buyback.

content_blocks.heading: Key Explanation
content_blocks.body:
ROE helps players compare business returns against safer instruments. If a business earns lower returns than low-risk alternatives, players should question why they should take equity risk.

However, ROE can be distorted by high debt, low equity, one-time profit, or accounting effects. Always connect ROE to balance sheet and cash flow.

Profit can be used for:

- Dividend
- Reinvestment / expansion
- Debt repayment
- Buyback
- Cash reserve

The ideal use depends on company opportunities and capital discipline.

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: Profit is not enough. Players must know how efficiently the company uses capital, how much profit belongs to each share, and how management uses profit.
ROE = Net Profit / Equity
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 09 Balance Sheet
sections.title: 09 Balance Sheet Basics: Assets, Liabilities, Equity
sections.completion_signal: You can use balance sheet basics: assets, liabilities, equity in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
A company has:

- Assets: Rp10T
- Liabilities: Rp3T
- Equity: ?
checks.question: What is the company equity?

options.label: Rp7T.
options.feedback: Correct. Assets = Liabilities + Equity, so Equity = Rp10T - Rp3T = Rp7T.

options.label: Rp3T.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Rp10T.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Rp13T.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: The Balance Sheet shows what the company owns, owes, and what belongs to shareholders.

content_blocks.heading: Core Equation
content_blocks.body: Assets = Liabilities + Equity

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Identify assets, liabilities, and equity.
- Distinguish current and non-current assets/liabilities.
- Understand equity as net worth of the company.
- Recognize retained earnings as accumulated profit kept in the business.

content_blocks.heading: Key Explanation
content_blocks.body:
| Category | Meaning | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Current Assets | Assets expected to become cash/useful within one year | Cash, receivables, inventory |
| Non-Current Assets | Long-term assets | Fixed assets, investments, mining assets, property |
| Current Liabilities | Obligations due within one year | Trade payables, short-term bank debt, taxes payable |
| Non-Current Liabilities | Obligations due after one year | Bonds, long-term bank loans |
| Equity | Owner's residual claim | Paid-in capital, retained earnings |

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: The Balance Sheet shows what the company owns, owes, and what belongs to shareholders.
Assets = Liabilities + Equity
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 10 Leverage
sections.title: 10 Leverage, DER, Gearing, and Capital Structure
sections.completion_signal: You can use leverage, der, gearing, and capital structure in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
| Company | DER | OCF | Revenue Stability | Interpretation |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|
| A | 0.3x | Positive | Stable | Low leverage |
| B | 3.0x | Positive | Long-term contracts | High leverage but may be acceptable |
| C | 5.0x | Negative | Declining | High danger |
checks.question: Which company has the highest leverage danger?

options.label: Company C.
options.feedback: Correct. High DER becomes more dangerous when operating cash flow is negative and revenue is declining.

options.label: Company A.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Company B.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: All three have equal leverage danger.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: Debt can help companies grow, but too much debt can make them fragile. Players must understand whether a company looks rich because of real equity growth or because it borrows aggressively.

content_blocks.heading: Formulas
content_blocks.body:
Debt to Equity Ratio = Total Liabilities / Equity
Gearing Ratio = Interest-Bearing Debt / Shareholder Equity

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Calculate DER and gearing ratio.
- Understand difference between total liabilities and interest-bearing debt.
- Interpret leverage based on industry context.
- Compare equity growth versus liability growth.

content_blocks.heading: Key Explanation
content_blocks.body:
Debt is not always bad. Some industries use more leverage because they have long-term contracts, predictable cash flow, or asset-heavy expansion. But excessive leverage becomes dangerous when:

- Revenue falls
- Operating profit weakens
- Interest expense rises
- Cash flow turns negative
- Debt matures when business is weak

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: Debt can help companies grow, but too much debt can make them fragile. Players must understand whether a company looks rich because of real equity growth or because it borrows aggressively.
Debt to Equity Ratio = Total Liabilities / Equity
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 11 Liquidity
sections.title: 11 Liquidity: Current Ratio and Cash Ratio
sections.completion_signal: You can use liquidity: current ratio and cash ratio in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
Company has:

- Current liabilities: Rp5B
- Cash: Rp2B
- Receivables: Rp3B
- Land: Rp100B
checks.question: Does the land help pay tomorrow's bills?

options.label: Not immediately. Land is valuable but not liquid.
options.feedback: Correct. Liquidity focuses on assets that can pay near-term obligations; land may be valuable but slow to convert into cash.

options.label: Yes. Land should be treated the same as cash.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Yes. Current liabilities disappear because total assets are high.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: No analysis is needed because land value is large.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: Liquidity measures whether a company can pay near-term obligations. A profitable company can still be in trouble if it cannot pay bills when due.

content_blocks.heading: Formulas
content_blocks.body:
Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities
Cash Ratio = Cash and Cash Equivalents / Current Liabilities

content_blocks.heading: General Guide
content_blocks.body:
| Ratio | Interpretation |
|---:|---|
| Current Ratio < 1 | Potential liquidity problem |
| Current Ratio > 1 | Generally safer |
| Current Ratio > 3 | May indicate idle assets, depending on business |
| Cash Ratio very low | Needs receivables/inventory conversion or refinancing |
| Cash Ratio very high | Safe, but may be inefficient if cash is idle |

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Calculate current ratio and cash ratio.
- Understand the difference between liquid and illiquid assets.
- Recognize short-term debt risk.
- Understand why large assets do not always mean easy payment ability.

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: Liquidity measures whether a company can pay near-term obligations. A profitable company can still be in trouble if it cannot pay bills when due.
Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 12 Working Capital
sections.title: 12 Working Capital: Receivables, Inventory, Payables, and CCC
sections.completion_signal: You can use working capital: receivables, inventory, payables, and ccc in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
A retailer reports:

- Revenue up 5%
- Inventory up 60%
- Gross margin down
checks.question: What is the working-capital concern?

options.label: Inventory may be piling up, demand may be weakening, and discounts may pressure margin.
options.feedback: Correct. Inventory rising much faster than revenue while margin falls can signal buildup, discounting, or weaker demand.

options.label: The company has no risk because revenue still grew.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Inventory growth always means demand is stronger.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Gross margin no longer matters once inventory rises.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: Working capital tells players whether money gets stuck in customers, inventory, or suppliers. A company can show revenue growth but fail to collect cash.

content_blocks.heading: Key Metrics
content_blocks.body:
| Metric | Meaning |
|---|---|
| DSO | How long customers take to pay |
| DIO | How long inventory stays before sold |
| DPO | How long company takes to pay suppliers |
| Cash Conversion Cycle | Time from cash going out to cash coming back |

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Understand DSO, DIO, DPO, and cash conversion cycle conceptually.
- Detect receivables growing faster than revenue.
- Detect inventory piling up.
- Understand supplier payment flexibility and risk.

content_blocks.heading: Key Explanation
content_blocks.body:
Red flags include:

- Receivables growing much faster than revenue
- Large receivables from related parties
- Receivables with unclear aging or weak collectability
- Inventory growing while sales slow down
- Payables stretched too far, damaging supplier relationships

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: Working capital tells players whether money gets stuck in customers, inventory, or suppliers. A company can show revenue growth but fail to collect cash.
| Metric | Meaning |
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 13 Red Flags
sections.title: 13 Balance Sheet Red Flags: Goodwill, Receivables, Inventory, Related Parties
sections.completion_signal: You can use balance sheet red flags: goodwill, receivables, inventory, related parties in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
A company has:

- Goodwill: 50% of equity
- Net profit down
- Acquisition target underperforming
checks.question: What is the key balance-sheet risk?

options.label: Potential goodwill impairment.
options.feedback: Correct. Large goodwill tied to an underperforming acquisition may need impairment and can weaken equity quality.

options.label: Guaranteed dividend growth.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: No risk because goodwill is always cash.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Inventory is definitely the only problem.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: Some Balance Sheet items require extra skepticism. Big numbers are not always strong assets. They can hide risk.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Understand why large goodwill can be risky.
- Identify inventory and receivables red flags.
- Understand related-party transaction risk.
- Learn when to check footnotes.

content_blocks.heading: Key Explanation
content_blocks.body:
| Red Flag | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| Large goodwill | Acquisition may fail and cause impairment loss |
| Inventory rising faster than sales | Products may not sell or may need discounting |
| Receivables rising faster than sales | Company may not collect cash |
| Related-party receivables | Money may be stuck within group entities |
| Unclear footnotes | Harder to judge asset quality |

Goodwill appears when a company buys another company above its net asset value. If expected synergy fails, goodwill may be impaired, reducing profit and equity.

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: Some Balance Sheet items require extra skepticism. Big numbers are not always strong assets. They can hide risk.
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 14 Cash Flow
sections.title: 14 Cash Flow Statement: OCF, ICF, and FCF
sections.completion_signal: You can use cash flow statement: ocf, icf, and fcf in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
| Company | OCF | ICF | Financing CF | Interpretation |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| A | +100 | -40 | -20 | Self-funded expansion and repayment/dividends |
| B | -20 | -100 | +130 | Growth funded by debt/equity, riskier |
| C | +50 | +70 | -30 | Selling assets, not necessarily growing |
checks.question: Which company has the healthier cash pattern?

options.label: Company A.
options.feedback: Correct. Positive OCF funding investment and shareholder/lender payments is healthier than growth dependent on external financing.

options.label: Company B.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Company C.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: All three are equally healthy.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: Cash flow reveals whether the business generates real money. Accounting profit can look good, but cash flow shows whether money actually comes in.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Understand operating, investing, and financing cash flow.
- Recognize why operating cash flow is critical.
- Understand when investing cash flow is healthy or dangerous.
- Interpret financing cash flow as money from/to lenders and shareholders.

content_blocks.heading: Key Explanation
content_blocks.body:
| Cash Flow Section | Meaning | Investor Question |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Cash Flow | Cash from core business | Does the business generate real cash? |
| Investing Cash Flow | Cash used for or received from long-term assets | Is the company expanding productively or selling assets? |
| Financing Cash Flow | Cash from debt/equity or paid to lenders/shareholders | Is growth self-funded or dependent on external money? |

Ideal early pattern for a healthy growing company:

OCF positive
ICF negative due to expansion
Financing depends on funding needs

But the source of expansion matters. If OCF is weak and ICF is huge, the company may depend on debt or equity issuance.

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: Cash flow reveals whether the business generates real money. Accounting profit can look good, but cash flow shows whether money actually comes in.
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 15 Profit vs Cash
sections.title: 15 Net Profit vs Operating Cash Flow
sections.completion_signal: You can use net profit vs operating cash flow in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
Company reports:

- Net profit: Rp1T
- OCF: -Rp500B
- Receivables: +80%
checks.question: What action should the player take?

options.label: Do not rely on the net profit headline; investigate receivables and cash collection.
options.feedback: Correct. High profit with negative OCF and rising receivables may mean earnings are not converting into cash.

options.label: Buy immediately because net profit is Rp1T.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Ignore operating cash flow because it is not part of profit.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Assume receivables growth is always good.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: Profit is accounting. Cash is survival. Players must compare net profit with OCF to judge quality.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Compare net profit and OCF.
- Detect red flags when profit is high but cash is low.
- Understand cases where net profit is low but OCF is still healthy.

content_blocks.heading: Key Explanation
content_blocks.body:
| Situation | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| Net profit high, OCF low/negative | Possible receivables, inventory, non-cash gain, or weak collection risk |
| Net profit low/negative, OCF positive | May be due to non-cash expense like depreciation; investigate further |
| Net profit and OCF both positive | Stronger profit quality |
| Net profit negative and OCF negative | High-risk unless early-stage turnaround or special context |

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: Profit is accounting. Cash is survival. Players must compare net profit with OCF to judge quality.
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 16 Footnotes
sections.title: 16 Notes to Financial Statements: The Business Kitchen
sections.completion_signal: You can use notes to financial statements: the business kitchen in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario: A company says revenue is diversified. Footnotes show 80% revenue comes from one customer.
checks.question: Is the diversification claim valid?

options.label: No. Customer concentration risk is high.
options.feedback: Correct. Footnotes can reveal concentration risk hidden behind broad management language.

options.label: Yes. Management said diversified, so footnotes do not matter.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Yes. One customer is always safer than many customers.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: It cannot be checked from notes or footnotes.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: The main statements show big numbers. The Notes to Financial Statements explain what is inside those numbers.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Understand why footnotes/CALK matter.
- Identify key information inside notes: revenue breakdown, COGS, OPEX, receivables aging, segments, backlog, related parties, commitments.
- Use footnotes to validate or challenge the headline numbers.

content_blocks.heading: Key Explanation
content_blocks.body:
Key items to check:

| Footnote Area | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| Revenue breakdown | Shows product, customer, or geographic source of sales |
| COGS breakdown | Shows biggest cost drivers |
| OPEX breakdown | Shows whether expenses are controlled |
| Receivables aging | Shows collectability risk |
| Related parties | Shows governance and cash-stuck risk |
| Operating segments | Shows which business line creates profit |
| Backlog | Shows future revenue visibility |
| Commitments | Shows future obligations |

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: The main statements show big numbers. The Notes to Financial Statements explain what is inside those numbers.
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 17 Backlog
sections.title: 17 Backlog and Operating Segments
sections.completion_signal: You can use backlog and operating segments in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
A company has two segments:

| Segment | Revenue Contribution | Operating Margin | Growth |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Coal | 80% | 35% | Declining |
| Renewable Energy | 20% | 10% | Growing 50% |
checks.question: What is the key thesis question?

options.label: Can the sunrise segment grow enough to offset decline in the dominant segment?
options.feedback: Correct. Segment analysis asks whether the growing business can become large and profitable enough to change the company thesis.

options.label: Only the renewable segment matters because it grows faster.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Only the coal segment matters because it is currently larger.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Segment mix is irrelevant once total revenue is known.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: Backlog and segment information help players understand future revenue visibility and which parts of the business actually create value.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Define backlog.
- Understand why backlog matters in project-based businesses.
- Identify dominant segments, segment margins, sunrise segments, and sunset segments.
- Understand concentration risk by segment, project, or customer.

content_blocks.heading: Key Explanation
content_blocks.body:
Backlog is work or contracts already obtained but not yet recognized as revenue. It is especially useful in sectors like construction, manufacturing, services, technology, or project-based businesses.

Backlog checklist:

- Backlog compared with annual revenue
- New order book trend
- Contract duration
- Recurring vs one-off contracts
- Client quality
- Client concentration
- Segment breakdown

Operating segments reveal:

- Which segment generates revenue
- Which segment generates profit
- Which segment is growing fastest
- Which segment is dragging performance
- Whether company risk is diversified or concentrated

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: Backlog and segment information help players understand future revenue visibility and which parts of the business actually create value.
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

sections.label: 18 Action Plan
sections.title: 18 Integrated Fundamental Analysis and Action Plan
sections.completion_signal: You can use integrated fundamental analysis and action plan in a basic business-quality read.

checks.title: Scenario Check
checks.scenario:
A company appears on the leaderboard because net profit rose 250%.

Facts:

- Revenue only grew 3%
- Gross margin fell from 30% to 22%
- OCF is negative
- Receivables rose 70%
- Net profit jump came from selling a subsidiary
- DER is 2.5x
- Interest coverage is 1.3x
- Footnotes show related-party receivables
checks.question: What is the best action plan?

options.label: Watchlist and investigate cash collection, non-recurring gain, debt, and related-party exposure.
options.feedback: Correct. The integrated read flags weak growth quality, falling margin, negative OCF, leverage, low interest coverage, and related-party risk.

options.label: Buy because net profit rose 250%.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: All-in because the headline looks good.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

options.label: Ignore all reports and follow price momentum.
options.feedback: Not quite. Use the scenario facts, not the headline, to make the fundamental decision.

content_blocks.heading: Core Concept
content_blocks.body: No company is perfect. Fundamental analysis is about judging whether the strengths are enough to compensate for the weaknesses at the right price.

content_blocks.heading: Learning Objectives
content_blocks.body:
Players should be able to:

- Combine Income Statement, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow Statement, and Footnotes.
- Identify green flags and red flags.
- Build an action plan: Buy, Watchlist, Avoid, or Investigate Further.
- Understand tolerance: not every weakness is fatal, but every weakness must be priced and understood.

content_blocks.heading: Integrated Framework
content_blocks.body:
| Area | Green Flag | Red Flag |
|---|---|---|
| Revenue | Stable or sustainable growth | One-time spike, declining trend |
| Gross Margin | Stable/improving, pricing power | Margin squeezed, COGS rising faster |
| Operating Margin | Efficient OPEX | OPEX grows faster than revenue |
| Net Profit | Recurring and supported by OCF | Boosted by one-time gain |
| ROE/ROA | Strong and consistent | High due to leverage or one-time profit |
| Debt | Manageable, covered by profit/cash | High DER + weak ICR + weak OCF |
| Liquidity | Current ratio healthy, cash adequate | Current ratio <1, cash ratio weak |
| Working Capital | Receivables/inventory controlled | Receivables/inventory ballooning |
| Cash Flow | OCF positive and consistent | CFO negative repeatedly |
| Footnotes | Transparent segment/backlog/customer info | Related-party risks, unclear details |

content_blocks.heading: Action Plan Rules
content_blocks.body:
| Result | Suggested Game Action |
|---|---|
| Strong fundamentals + reasonable price | Candidate for buy/research deeper |
| Strong business but expensive | Watchlist |
| Mixed fundamentals + clear catalyst | Small position or deeper investigation |
| Weak fundamentals + high hype | Avoid or speculative-only with strict sizing |
| Red flags unresolved | Avoid until clarified |

content_blocks.title: Lesson recap
bullets: No company is perfect. Fundamental analysis is about judging whether the strengths are enough to compensate for the weaknesses at the right price.
| Area | Green Flag | Red Flag |
Use the scenario to turn financial statement evidence into an action plan.

bullets: Compare growth with margin quality.

sections.label: 20 Glossary
sections.title: 20 Fundamental Glossary
sections.completion_signal: Use glossary search to review financial statement terms.

pages.heading: Fundamental vocabulary
pages.body: Search for key financial statement terms such as Revenue, Gross Profit, OCF, DER, Current Ratio, Backlog, and Related Party.

content_blocks.heading: How to use this glossary
content_blocks.body: Use glossary search whenever a financial term blocks your analysis. The goal is to connect each word to an investor question, not to memorize accounting jargon.

quiz_questions.prompt: Revenue growth from Year 1 to Year 2 is:
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Revenue rose from Rp10T to Rp12T, a 20 percent increase.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Check the revenue line: (12 - 10) / 10 = 20 percent.

quiz_questions.prompt: Gross margin in Year 1 is:
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Year 1 gross margin is Rp4T / Rp10T = 40 percent.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Use gross profit divided by revenue for Year 1.

quiz_questions.prompt: Gross margin in Year 2 is:
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Year 2 gross margin is Rp3.6T / Rp12T = 30 percent.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Use gross profit divided by revenue for Year 2.

quiz_questions.prompt: What happened to gross margin?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Gross margin declined from 40 percent to 30 percent.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Compare Year 1 margin with Year 2 margin before judging growth quality.

quiz_questions.prompt: Interest coverage in Year 2 is approximately:
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Operating profit Rp1.6T divided by interest expense Rp0.8T is about 2.0x.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Interest coverage compares operating profit with interest expense.

quiz_questions.prompt: DER in Year 2 is approximately:
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Liabilities of Rp8T divided by equity of Rp6.5T is about 1.2x.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: DER compares liabilities with equity.

quiz_questions.prompt: What is the biggest concern with Year 2 net profit?

options.label: It is boosted by one-time gain

quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Year 2 profit is heavily boosted by a one-time gain.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Look below the headline net profit and check whether it repeats.

quiz_questions.prompt: Receivables rising from Rp1T to Rp2.5T while OCF turns negative may indicate:

options.label: Collection risk or aggressive credit sales

quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Receivables rising while OCF turns negative can signal collection risk.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Receivables are promises; operating cash flow is the cash reality check.

quiz_questions.prompt: Inventory rising from Rp1.5T to Rp2.8T while gross margin declines may indicate:

options.label: Possible inventory buildup or weakening demand

quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Rising inventory plus weaker margin can point to buildup or weaker demand.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Inventory risk grows when stock piles up while pricing power weakens.

quiz_questions.prompt: Best action plan:

options.label: Buy immediately because net profit rose
Investigate deeper; profit quality, working capital, leverage, and cash flow are concerning

quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The case needs deeper investigation before any buy decision.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Headline net profit is not enough when cash flow, working capital, leverage, and one-time gains are flashing yellow or red.

categories.label: Corporate Action
categories.summary: Learn how company meetings, dividends, capital actions, control deals, and leadership resets change a stock.

badge.label: Corporate Action Basics
badge.description: Passed the Corporate Action Academy foundation quiz.

sections.label: 01 Overview
sections.title: 01 Reading Corporate Actions
sections.completion_signal: You can identify whether an event is a meeting, information session, cash/share distribution, financing action, control deal, or governance reset.

pages.heading: What is a corporate action?
pages.body: A corporate action is a company-originated event that changes investor rights, cash flows, share count, ownership, listing status, or leadership expectations. In the game these events come from companies, boards, controllers, offerors, or creditors. A normal player can react to them, research them, attend eligible meetings, vote with record-date shares, or ask contacts for a read.

pages.heading: Meetings and calls
pages.body: Annual RUPS is the routine yearly shareholder meeting. RUPSLB is an extraordinary shareholder meeting for a specific agenda such as financing, buybacks, control changes, restructuring, or leadership. Earnings calls are information sessions tied to reported results and do not give the player agenda control.

pages.heading: Player agency
pages.body: The key rule is ownership. Buying shares can give attendance, voting, dividend, tender, or rights eligibility depending on the record date. It does not let the player directly steer company strategy unless a future system gives the player majority or control ownership.

checks.question: Who normally initiates corporate-action direction in this game?

options.label: Company, board, controller, offeror, or creditor
options.feedback: Correct. The player reads and reacts unless ownership control gives stronger influence later.

options.label: Any player from the order ticket
options.feedback: No. Normal players cannot directly schedule company-direction meetings.

options.label: The price chart by itself
options.feedback: Charts can reveal reaction, but the action itself comes from the company-side event chain.

content_blocks.title: Implemented event venues
bullets: Annual RUPS: routine shareholder meeting, often used for dividend and governance agenda items.
RUPSLB: extraordinary shareholder meeting for specific corporate-action proposals.
Earnings call: management explains reported results and outlook without giving voting control.

content_blocks.heading: Record date discipline
content_blocks.body: Eligibility depends on the snapshot date attached to the action. Selling after the record date does not erase captured rights, while buying after the record date does not create them.

sections.label: 02 Dividends
sections.title: 02 Cash and Stock Dividends
sections.completion_signal: You can explain how cash dividends and stock dividends affect cash, share count, reference price, and record-date eligibility.

pages.heading: Cash dividend
pages.body: A cash dividend pays money to eligible shareholders. In the game it moves through approval, ex-date, record, and payment timing. The stock can adjust when it goes ex-dividend because the cash claim has separated from the share.

pages.heading: Stock dividend
pages.body: A stock dividend grants bonus shares instead of cash. The player's eligible share count receives additional shares on payment, while company share count and historical reference prices adjust to keep the economics consistent.

pages.heading: How to read the setup
pages.body: Check payout size, yield, timetable, and whether you held shares on the record date. A high dividend is not automatically bullish if the business is weak or the payout is unsustainable.

checks.question: What matters most for dividend eligibility?

options.label: Shares captured on the record date
options.feedback: Correct. Eligibility follows the record-date registry.

options.label: Buying any time before payment
options.feedback: Payment comes later, but eligibility is already captured by the record date.

options.label: The highest price paid
options.feedback: Your cost basis does not decide eligibility.

content_blocks.title: Implemented dividend actions
bullets: Cash dividend: eligible holders receive cash on the payment date.
Stock dividend: eligible holders receive bonus shares and the reference price/share count adjusts.

sections.label: 03 Capital Structure
sections.title: 03 Financing, Buybacks, and Splits
sections.completion_signal: You can separate dilution, float absorption, and cosmetic share-count changes.

pages.heading: Rights issue
pages.body: A rights issue offers new shares to existing holders, usually at a discount. Eligible holders receive an entitlement. If they exercise, they add shares and spend cash; if they cannot or do not exercise, their ownership can be diluted.

pages.heading: Private placement
pages.body: A private placement sells new shares to a targeted investor. It can raise cash faster than a rights issue, but it can dilute existing holders and may reduce free float if the new investor is locked or strategic.

pages.heading: Stock buyback
pages.body: A stock buyback authorizes the company to buy and retire shares. Execution can reduce free float and support price, but it also spends corporate cash and can be questioned if the business needs funding.

pages.heading: Stock split
pages.body: A stock split changes the number of shares and reference price. A normal split can improve trading accessibility; a reverse split can reset a very low nominal price. By itself, a split does not create business value.

checks.question: Which action most directly creates new shares for financing?

options.label: Rights issue or private placement
options.feedback: Correct. Both are new-share financing paths.

options.label: Stock buyback
options.feedback: A buyback usually retires or absorbs shares rather than issuing new ones.

options.label: Earnings call
options.feedback: An earnings call explains results; it does not issue shares.

content_blocks.title: Implemented capital-structure actions
bullets: Rights issue: shareholder entitlement, exercise price, dilution, and TERP-style adjustment.
Private placement: targeted investor issuance, dilution, proceeds, and free-float effect.
Stock buyback: authorization, execution ratio, retired shares, float reduction, and price support.
Stock split: split or reverse-split ratio, holding adjustment, share-count adjustment, and reference-price reset.

sections.label: 04 Control and Turnaround
sections.title: 04 Control Deals, Rescues, and Leadership
sections.completion_signal: You can read whether a corporate action is changing control, rescuing the balance sheet, transforming the listing, or resetting leadership.

pages.heading: Tender offer
pages.body: A tender offer lets holders choose whether to sell into an offer. In interactive meetings the player can tender, hold, or observe if eligible. A successful offer can shrink float, create public-float warnings, or eventually cash out remaining holders in a go-private branch.

pages.heading: Strategic merger or acquisition
pages.body: A strategic M&A deal can cash out the target at an acquisition price, disable public trading for the acquired listing, and turn the question into premium versus closing risk.

pages.heading: Backdoor listing
pages.body: A backdoor listing changes control and injects a new business into an existing listed shell. It can rewrite company identity, dilute old holders, create sponsor lock-up risk, and spawn follow-on financing or milestone events.

pages.heading: Restructuring and CEO change
pages.body: Restructuring is a rescue package around debt relief, asset sales, debt conversion, and liquidity penalties. CEO change is a leadership reset that can shift execution traits and market confidence while preserving the listing.

checks.question: What is the best first read on a backdoor listing?

options.label: Control change plus injected asset quality
options.feedback: Correct. The sponsor, asset quality, dilution, and post-deal milestones matter together.

options.label: Only whether the ticker survives
options.feedback: Ticker survival matters, but the economic change comes from control, dilution, and the injected asset.

options.label: Only the meeting vote button
options.feedback: The vote matters, but the terms and aftermath determine the trade read.

content_blocks.title: Implemented control and turnaround actions
bullets: Tender offer: shareholder election, offer premium, float absorption, public-float warning, and go-private branch.
Strategic merger / acquisition: cash-out premium, target acquisition, delisting/trading block, and final holder treatment.
Backdoor listing: control change, asset injection, identity rewrite, dilution, sponsor lock-up, follow-on rights, and milestones.
Restructuring: debt relief, asset sale, debt-to-equity conversion, liquidity penalty, dilution, and stress overhang.
CEO change: leadership slate, CEO replacement, execution/story trait nudge, and market reaction.

sections.label: 05 Quiz
sections.title: Corporate Action Basics Quiz
sections.completion_signal: Score 80 percent or better to earn Corporate Action Basics.

sections.label: 06 Glossary
sections.title: Glossary
sections.completion_signal: Use this any time you need a quick definition.

quiz_questions.prompt: Why can a normal player ask Network contacts for information, but not directly request RUPSLB direction?

options.label: Contacts provide information; company direction comes from company-side control.
Contacts force all shareholders to vote with the player.

quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Right. Contacts improve reads; they do not hand the player corporate control.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Review the overview. The player can research and react, but normal corporate direction is not an order-ticket command.

quiz_questions.prompt: A dividend or meeting says eligibility is based on record-date shares. What does that mean?

options.label: The game uses the shares captured on that snapshot date.

quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Record-date snapshots drive eligibility.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Record date is the eligibility snapshot, separate from later payment or meeting timing.

quiz_questions.prompt: Which pair should make you think about dilution first?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Both create new shares for financing.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Look for new-share issuance when you are checking dilution risk.

quiz_questions.prompt: Which action can rewrite the listed company's identity while keeping the ticker alive?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Right. Backdoor listings model control change, asset injection, and identity rewrite.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Review the control and turnaround lesson. Identity rewrite belongs to backdoor listings.

quiz_questions.prompt: What is the player's main decision in an eligible tender-offer meeting?

options.label: Tender shares, hold shares, or observe

quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Tender offers are shareholder elections around an offer.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Tender offers ask holders whether to sell into the offer, hold, or observe.

categories.summary: Learn the basics of technical analysis and apply that knowledge by making a thinking framework.

badge.label: Technical Basics
badge.description: Passed the Technical Academy foundation quiz.

sections.label: 01 Intro
sections.title: 01 Getting to Know Technical Analysis
sections.completion_signal: You can explain that TA studies price and volume, then uses probability rather than prediction.

pages.heading: What is technical analysis?
pages.body: Technical analysis evaluates securities by analyzing statistical data generated by market activity, primarily price and volume. Unlike fundamental analysis (which reads financial statements), TA focuses on chart patterns and price behavior to anticipate future price direction.

pages.heading: TA vs Fundamental: how they work together
pages.body: Fundamental analysis tells you whether a company is worth buying. Technical analysis tells you when to buy it. It is wise to combine both, use TA for execution discipline. Never use TA alone without understanding what the business does.

pages.heading: IDX context
pages.body: IDX (Bursa Efek Indonesia) has over 900 listed companies and a rapidly growing retail investor base. Short-term price moves are heavily sentiment-driven, making TA especially relevant. The most significant price moves tend to occur in the first and last 30 minutes of each trading session. IDX trading hours: pre-opening 08:45–09:00 WIB, session I 09:00–12:00, session II 13:30–16:00.

pages.heading: Common Mistake
pages.body: Do not add indicators before reading price action. Indicators can confirm a view, but they should not replace structure.

checks.question: Which data does Technical Analysis read most directly?

options.label: Price and volume
options.feedback: Right. TA begins with price behavior and participation.

options.label: Director biography
options.feedback: That can matter for fundamentals, but it is not the main TA input.

options.label: Dividend policy only
options.feedback: Dividends can affect a thesis, but TA is built from price and volume.

checks.question: What is the healthiest way to use TA?

options.label: Predict exact future prices
options.feedback: TA should not be treated as certainty.

options.label: Frame probabilities and risk
options.feedback: Correct. The point is better decisions under uncertainty.

options.label: Ignore invalidation if the pattern looks nice
options.feedback: A nice pattern without invalidation is still a risky guess.

sections.label: 02 Market Structure
sections.title: Market Structure
sections.completion_signal: You can identify whether a chart is trending, ranging, broken, or worth deeper analysis.

pages.heading: Concept
pages.body: Structure is the market's skeleton. Bullish structure often makes higher highs and higher lows. Bearish structure often makes lower highs and lower lows. Sideways structure moves inside a range.

pages.heading: Chart Cue
pages.body: Mark support and resistance as areas, not exact magic lines. Look for bases, trendline breaks, accumulation, distribution, and whether the bigger timeframe agrees.

pages.heading: How To Read It
pages.body: Begin from the bigger timeframe. If daily structure is healthy, a lower timeframe can help with precision. If daily structure is broken, a lower timeframe bounce may still be only noise.

pages.heading: Common Mistake
pages.body: Avoid calling every dip a support and every green candle a breakout. Structure needs location, follow-through, and volume context.

checks.question: A chart keeps printing higher highs and higher lows. What structure is this?

options.label: Uptrend
options.feedback: Correct. Higher highs and higher lows are classic bullish structure.

options.label: Downtrend
options.feedback: Downtrends usually print lower highs and lower lows.

options.label: No structure can be read
options.feedback: Structure can often be read from swing behavior.

checks.question: Why mark support and resistance as areas?

options.label: Because the exact line always holds
options.feedback: Exact lines are fragile; markets often test zones.

options.label: Because price often reacts around zones
options.feedback: Right. Areas reduce false precision.

options.label: So invalidation is never needed
options.feedback: You still need invalidation.

sections.label: 03 Candlesticks
sections.title: Candlesticks
sections.completion_signal: You can read OHLC, body, wick, timeframe context, and confirmation before acting on candles.

pages.heading: Concept
pages.body: A candle compresses open, high, low, and close into one visual. The body shows the open-close fight. The wick shows rejected prices.

pages.heading: Chart Cue
pages.body: Single-candle patterns include doji variants, shooting star, hammer, inverted hammer, marubozu, and spinning top. Multi-candle patterns include engulfing, harami, morning star, evening star, three white soldiers, and three black crows.

pages.heading: How To Read It
pages.body: A candle pattern matters more at a key area than in the middle of nowhere. Confirmation can come from the next candle, structure, volume, or a broader setup.

pages.heading: Common Mistake
pages.body: Do not buy every hammer or sell every doji. A candle is a clue, not a full trade plan.

pattern_cards.label: Hammer
pattern_cards.cue: Long lower wick near support; watch for follow-through.

pattern_cards.label: Shooting Star
pattern_cards.cue: Long upper wick near resistance; watch for rejection.

pattern_cards.label: Engulfing
pattern_cards.cue: Second candle overwhelms prior body; context decides meaning.

pattern_cards.label: Doji
pattern_cards.cue: Indecision; strongest when it appears at a meaningful area.

checks.question: A long upper wick near resistance usually suggests what?

options.label: Buying was rejected at higher prices
options.feedback: Correct. The wick shows a failed push higher.

options.label: A guaranteed breakout tomorrow
options.feedback: No single candle guarantees a breakout.

options.label: Context no longer matters
options.feedback: Context still matters most.

checks.question: What makes a candle pattern safer to act on?

options.label: A bigger chart label
options.feedback: Labels do not create edge.

options.label: Confirmation from area, volume, or follow-through
options.feedback: Right. Candles need confirmation.

options.label: Entering before the candle closes
options.feedback: Early entries carry extra fakeout risk.

sections.label: 04 Patterns
sections.title: Patterns
sections.completion_signal: You can validate a pattern by structure, location, volume, and invalidation.

pages.heading: Concept
pages.body: Patterns are repeated structures that show how supply and demand are changing. Useful patterns include range or box, VCP, double bottom, cup and handle, head and shoulders, and ascending triangle.

pages.heading: Chart Cue
pages.body: A valid setup should be visible in structure, appear in a sensible location, show volume behavior that supports the story, and have a clear invalidation point.

pages.heading: How To Read It
pages.body: For a breakout, look for tightening volatility, pressure near resistance, and volume expansion. For reversal patterns, look for failed breakdowns, higher lows, or clear rejection.

pages.heading: Common Mistake
pages.body: Do not force a famous pattern onto messy price action. If the setup is unclear, no trade is also a decision.

pattern_cards.label: VCP
pattern_cards.cue: Swings tighten as selling pressure dries up.

pattern_cards.label: Double Bottom
pattern_cards.cue: Second low holds; confirmation comes through neckline strength.

pattern_cards.label: Cup and Handle
pattern_cards.cue: Rounded recovery followed by a smaller consolidation.

pattern_cards.label: Head and Shoulders
pattern_cards.cue: Trend weakens through lower right shoulder and neckline break.

checks.question: Which set best validates a chart pattern?

options.label: A famous pattern name only
options.feedback: The name alone is not enough.

options.label: Structure, location, volume, and invalidation
options.feedback: Correct. That is the checklist.

options.label: One green candle anywhere
options.feedback: A single candle without context is weak.

checks.question: What should you do when the pattern is unclear?

options.label: Force the closest pattern name
options.feedback: Forcing patterns creates false confidence.

options.label: Wait or skip until structure is clearer
options.feedback: Good. No trade can be the cleanest trade.

options.label: Buy more so the pattern works
options.feedback: Position size does not fix an unclear setup.

sections.label: 05 Moving Average
sections.title: Moving Average
sections.completion_signal: You can use moving averages as context and confirmation, not as blind buy or sell buttons.

pages.heading: Concept
pages.body: Moving averages smooth price. SMA gives equal weight, EMA reacts faster, and WMA weights recent prices. All of them lag because they use past data.

pages.heading: Chart Cue
pages.body: Common periods include 5, 10, 20, 60, 100, and 200. Slope can show trend quality, while MA 200 often acts as a long-term context line.

pages.heading: How To Read It
pages.body: Use MA slope, dynamic support or resistance, and confluence with structure. Golden cross and death cross are context signals, not automatic orders.

pages.heading: Common Mistake
pages.body: Do not buy only because price touches an MA. Ask whether structure, volume, and risk still make sense.

pages.heading: MACD Note
pages.body: MACD can confirm momentum with crossovers, improving histogram, or divergence. It should support your read, not replace it.

checks.question: Why are moving averages called lagging indicators?

options.label: They are calculated from past prices
options.feedback: Correct. They smooth history, so they respond after price changes.

options.label: They know tomorrow's close
options.feedback: No indicator knows tomorrow.

options.label: They only use volume
options.feedback: Moving averages are price-based unless stated otherwise.

sections.label: 06 Thinking Framework
sections.title: Thinking Framework
sections.completion_signal: You can run the 6-step technical routine without guessing.

pages.heading: Concept
pages.body: The routine is: start with context, mark key areas, identify the setup, add confirmation, build the trade plan, then execute and review.

pages.heading: Chart Cue
pages.body: For JPFA-style practice, read the daily context first, then use 1H structure for a cup-and-handle style precision entry. For SSIA-style practice, watch support near MA 200, doji confirmation, and MACD divergence.

pages.heading: How To Read It
pages.body: The framework keeps you from jumping straight to a signal. Every trade needs context, area, setup, confirmation, risk, and review.

pages.heading: Common Mistake
pages.body: Skipping the plan step turns analysis into impulse. Know entry, invalidation, target area, and position size before clicking buy.

checks.question: What is the first step in the 6-step framework?

options.label: Start with context
options.feedback: Correct. Big-picture context comes first.

options.label: Execute immediately
options.feedback: Execution comes after the plan.

options.label: Add every indicator
options.feedback: Indicators are confirmation tools, not the starting point.

quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Yes. Price and volume are the base inputs.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Review Intro: TA starts with price and volume.

options.label: TA removes the need for stops

quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. TA is probabilistic.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: TA is a probability tool, not a crystal ball.

quiz_questions.prompt: What should be read before indicators?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Right. Price action first.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: The routine starts with price action and structure.

quiz_questions.prompt: Higher highs and higher lows usually describe what?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Higher highs and higher lows point to bullish structure.

quiz_questions.prompt: Support and resistance should usually be marked as...
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Areas reduce false precision.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Support and resistance behave more like areas than exact lines.

quiz_questions.prompt: Why start from the bigger timeframe?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Right. Context first, precision later.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Bigger timeframe context keeps small moves in perspective.

quiz_questions.prompt: A candle body shows the relationship between...
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: The body is built from open and close.

quiz_questions.prompt: A long upper wick near resistance can suggest...
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: A long upper wick often shows rejection, especially near resistance.

quiz_questions.prompt: Which answer is a multi-candle pattern?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Engulfing uses more than one candle.

quiz_questions.prompt: What should you do before acting on a candle signal?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Candle clues need context and confirmation.

quiz_questions.prompt: A good pattern should include which validation set?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Pattern validation needs structure, location, volume, and invalidation.

quiz_questions.prompt: VCP mainly looks for...

options.label: Tightening volatility as selling pressure dries up

quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: VCP is about contractions and pressure drying up.

quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: If it is unclear, waiting is a valid decision.
